data.title: Qualcomm Remains A Buy Due To Edge AI Leadership
data.description:
It has been quite a while since I last covered Qualcomm Incorporated (QCOM). Back in mid-February, I reiterated my buy rating on the stock, citing undervaluation and their strong position in edge AI. Since then, Qualcomm hasn't been on a particularly strong run, as declines are at around 10%.

In the below analysis, it is shown that fiscal Q3 results were generally quite respectable, but guidance for Q4 is very soft. While this may justify the low level in the forward P/E ratio, I believe with the company being a leader in edge AI, the current valuation offers opportunity to long-term investors that can wait out the consumer headwinds. Therefore, I reiterate my Buy rating on Qualcomm stock.

An Edge AI Leader

Before taking a look at their financial metrics, I would like to start with a brief discussion on some of their most important announcements in their FY2025 Q3. Firstly, the company announced an extension of their collaboration with Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF). They stated that Xiaomi will continue to use Snapdragon 8 for their premium phones and that the company "will be one of the first to adopt the next-generation premium Snapdragon 8-series to be announced later this year, in China and globally." Moving forward, Qualcomm and Xiaomi's collaboration could widen to more edge products like automotive and AR/VR. With Xiaomi being a major player in consumer electronics in China, I believe the continuation and deepening of their collaboration should further boost Qualcomm as one of the world's leaders in edge AI.

Another collaboration that I believe is worth mentioning is the one with Advantech that was announced at COMPUTEX 2025. Qualcomm stated that together they aim to "advance AI-driven IoT applications" and "accelerate] the deployment of intelligent solutions across industries." This shows that the company's edge ambitions are beyond just consumer products and that the market is larger than perhaps previously thought. In my view, these two major collaborations show off the company's desire to maintain their strong position in this space as edge AI becomes increasingly important.

Now, let's move on to their financial results.

Top Line Growth Is Slowing

Qualcomm reported their FY2025 Q3 earnings near the end of July, and top line results may be slightly worrying in my view. They generated a total of $10.37 billion in revenue, representing a 10.35% increase over the prior year period. Revenues dropped QoQ for the second straight quarter, and the YoY growth rate slowed for the third consecutive quarter. This shows that perhaps consumer weakness is weighing on demand for consumer electronics. Still, they were able to edge past expectations by $12.26 million, no pun intended. Their QCT segment was the leader for growth, as they saw a 11% increase in revenues versus their QTL segment's 4% increase.

Within their QCT segment, IoT growth remained quite strong vs. Q2 but handsets and automotive growth both slowed considerably as another hint that consumers may be cutting back on discretionary spending. As a whole, I would say that top line results show that despite long-term edge AI tailwinds, economic headwinds are taking a toll on the company.

Margins Still Expand

Despite the slowing of top-line growth, there were positive developments in Qualcomm's margins. For their QCT segment, they reported an EBT margin of 30%, an expansion from 27% in the year ago quarter, while for their QTL segment, they saw an increase from 70% to 71% YoY. As you can see in the above charts, both EBIT margin and net profit margin were notably higher than in the prior year period, and so there was an increase in profitability overall. Do note, however, that when you go and look at their income statement, it is shown that a notable contributor to this increase in margin is a decrease in R&D spending by 1.46% to $2.226 billion. While this causes near term gain, it may lower their long-term growth prospects, and so that's something investors should keep in mind.

For the quarter, Qualcomm reported an EPS of $2.77, up 18.88% YoY. This is a modest acceleration from Q1's 16.80% growth rate and far outpaces revenue growth due to the margin expansion. This EPS figure also beat consensus expectations by a narrow margin of $0.06 to show slight outperformance. Overall, profitability of the business was quite robust, especially considering that demand seems to be a concern.

Buybacks Soar

As shown above, Qualcomm's cash generation was a bit weak even as EPS growth was strong. They reported operating cash flows of $2.875 billion, representing a 5.8% decline. Despite this, shareholders should have been happy that capital returns increased significantly, mainly as a result of share repurchases. Total dividend payout increased from $949 million to $968 million while buybacks soared from $1.585 billion to $3.118 billion. Considering that the stock's dividend yield is currently 2.28%, if Qualcomm continues to buyback stock at this rate, shareholders could see their returns swell. With cash generation not being particularly strong, it could be hard to keep buying back stock at this pace moving forward, however.

Q4 Guidance Is Soft

Qualcomm provided guidance for their FY2025 Q4. For revenues, they expect a total of between $10.3 billion and $11.1 billion. At the midpoint, this implies a YoY growth rate of just 4.45%. This is a slowdown from Q3's 10.35% growth rate and would also represent the fourth consecutive slowdown in YoY growth. Clearly, economic headwinds are expected to continue pressuring consumer demand for discretionary electronics. For adjusted EPS, they are expecting a range of $2.75 to $2.95. At the midpoint, this would imply a 5.95% increase YoY. This is a sharp slowdown from Q3's 18.88% growth rate. Furthermore, the fact that EPS growth is not expected to outpace revenue growth by much shows that the margin improvements that I discussed earlier aren't expected to continue happening in their Q4. As a whole, guidance for Q4 was very soft and shows that the outlook is rather unfavorable.

Valuation May Be Attractive

The forward P/E ratio for Qualcomm sits at a low-level relative to the past couple of years. The current reading of 13.05 in the multiple potentially provides opportunity in my view. For Q3, EPS results were quite strong as growth accelerated. However, as discussed just a moment ago, the outlook for the company looks rather concerning, as growth is expected to drop sharply. While this may justify a low level in the forward P/E, I believe for investors that have faith in the long-term potential of the company, this valuation could be attractive.

Recall that I started off this analysis with a discussion on their partnerships with Xiaomi and Advantech. Through these collaborations and other innovative efforts, Qualcomm is very likely to remain a leader in edge AI. The weak near-term guidance is likely merely a reflection of poor consumer confidence, and so in the long term, I expect the company to be an important piece of the AI industry.

With that in mind, note that the IT sector's GAAP and non-GAAP forward P/Es are 28.99 and 23.21, respectively. Therefore, Qualcomm is at a massive discount to the sector, and the valuation offers opportunity for long-term investors that believe the company is going to grow with the edge AI market.

Takeaway

In QCOM's fiscal Q3 results, there were some signs of consumer weakness in the form of slowing top line growth. However, an expansion of margin meant that profitability was strong. In their Q4 guidance, it was shown that the business environment is continuing to deteriorate with a sharp EPS growth slowdown expected. While this may justify a depressed level in the forward P/E ratio, as stated above, I believe the company's collaborations and AI advancements are likely to keep them in a leadership position for edge AI. With that being the case, I expect the valuation to recover once economic headwinds dissipate, and so for long-term investors, this may be an attractive entry point. As a result, I have decided to reiterate my buy rating on Qualcomm stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Vista Energy: Fast Growth And Excellent Profit Margin Will Create Value Despite High Debt Level
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I grant Vista Energy, S.A.B. de C.V. (NYSE:VIST) a buy rating. I am of the opinion that the company has an excellent growth outlook and possesses a high profit margin. In addition, it has competitive advantages compared to other oil companies.

The Argentinian government needs financial results to strengthen its international reserves. Vista is one of those companies that provides foreign reserves to the Argentinian economy. As such, it aligns with the Argentinian government to bring hard currency to the country. The second competitive advantage is that Vista Energy has a higher profit margin than most of the fracking companies in the Permian Basin. In addition, Vista's acquisition of Petronas E&P Argentina S.A. (PEPASA) is expected to improve its profit margin, as PEPASA has a higher profit margin. Furthermore, my estimation suggests that Vista could increase its revenues and adjusted EBITDA by approximately 95% in 2025 compared to 2024.

Vista's high debt level is the main risk factor. The company has a 125.84% debt-to-equity ratio, the highest ratio among some relevant fracking firms. Further, Vista's operations are located in Argentina, a country that has defaulted on its sovereign debt on nine occasions. As such, Argentina could experience another crisis in the upcoming years if the country cannot solve its macroeconomic imbalances. When taking all of these aspects into consideration, I still consider Vista to be an excellent investment opportunity.

Motivation

Since Seeking Alpha published my first article, Vista's stock price has declined by 14.57% due to lower oil prices, as depicted in the following chart. However, I still believe the company is poised to experience a significant increase in its stock price in the upcoming years.

However, in the short term, I think the company's stock price could continue to decrease. Oil prices are declining, but this fact does not mean that all energy firms will experience the same fate. Vista is one of those companies that could thrive even in a scenario of $60 per WTI oil price. Of course, if oil prices drop even further, Vista could experience headwinds.

Breakeven

Thanks to the significant increase in the Permian Basin oil production, the U.S. became the largest oil producer. Due to the Permian Basin's importance in the global oil market, I think oil prices will not drop below the breakeven price of Permian Basin oil producers for an extended period. If oil prices experience a considerable decline, the oil production in that location will experience a significant decrease. Ultimately, this drop will rebalance the global oil supply and demand, pushing oil prices up.

The Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas estimates that the median breakeven price to drill a new well in the Permian goes from $61 to $70 per WTI oil price per barrel ((depending on the location)). The current WTI oil price is $62.80 per barrel, which is approaching the previous lower band.

Moving to Vista, the following table shows the breakeven price of the company. I calculated this cost as the total expenses to produce a barrel of oil before taxes. The table indicates that the breakeven price of Vista is around $40. As such, the company could be profitable even if oil prices drop below the breakeven price in the Permian Basin.

In my view, Vista Energy's breakeven price is one of the company's most important aspects. There is a natural barrier, which is the high breakeven prices of the Permian oil producers. For instance, if the WTI oil price declines to $50 per barrel, many oil producers in the Permian Basin will be forced to reduce their capital expenditure, since they will be unprofitable. However, Vista Energy will generate profit even at that level.

CEO and Other Executives

The management team of Vista is formed by ex-YPF executives. YPF Sociedad Anónima (YPF) is the largest oil and gas company in Argentina, and it is controlled by the Government. With the previous explanation, the key point is that Vista has had excellent relationships with the different Argentinian governments, which is a positive aspect. In addition, the company has an excellent management team.

I begin with a brief description of Vista's CEO, Miguel Galuccio. Shortly after obtaining his degree in 1994, Galuccio joined YPF. In that company, he was promoted to various key roles as part of YPF's expansion plan. Subsequently, in 1999, he left YPF and joined Schlumberger. In Schlumberger, he also stood out and was promoted to different roles. The most relevant position that Galuccio achieved was director of production management in Schlumberger. In 2012, the ex-President of Argentina, Cristina Fernández, re-nationalized YPF and named Galuccio as the CEO of this company. In 2016, Galuccio resigned as CEO of YPF when the next Argentinian President, Mauricio Macri, took office.

When Galuccio finished his role in YPF, he founded Vista Energy in 2017. Due to his excellent performance in the oil industry, to some extent, Vista Energy received a concession from the Province of Neuquén, Argentina, to produce oil and gas in Bajada del Palo Oeste block and Bajada del Palo Este block in 2018. Both blocks accounted for 84.14% of the company's proved net reserves in 2024. In addition, other executives of Vista also come from YPF. In fact, the CFO, Chief Technology Officer, and other key executives held relevant positions in YPF. To conclude, the excellent management team of Vista and its relationship with the Argentinian government are competitive advantages.

Argentina International Reserves

The Argentinian government has conducted an economic plan to correct the macroeconomic distortion of the South American nation. The country reported fiscal deficits for 14 years. These deficits eroded the international reserves of Argentina. In 2018, the country had $66.22 billion in international reserves, declining to $29.59 billion in 2024. Due to the low reserves and other macroeconomic imbalances, the government has promoted reforms to attract investment and strengthen the country's international reserves. One of these reforms is the REGI, which incentivizes investments over $200 million. This law has benefited Vista Energy. The critical point is that Argentina needs financial resources to increase its international reserves. As such, it is promoting private companies with high export potential. For instance, in 2024, Vista sold $748 million of oil to the international market, which was more than half of its oil revenues. Therefore, Vista aligns with the Argentinian government's interests. As such, for the government, it is important that Vista Energy continues to expand its operations.

Pipeline

The Vaca Muerta Sur Oil Pipeline could boost Vista's revenues and earnings. The pipeline will connect Vaca Muerta with the Argentinian Atlantic coast. As such, Argentinian oil will have easier access to the international market. Ultimately, the ratio between the WTI oil price and the Argentinian oil price will decline. A lower ratio indicates that the South American nation will be selling its oil production at a better price compared to the WTI oil price.

For instance, in Canada, the Trans Mountain Pipeline has contributed to reducing the ratio WTI-to-WCS ((WCS is the Canadian oil benchmark)). This oil pipeline connects Edmonton, Alberta, Canada, to the Pacific Coast of that country. Since the inauguration, the ratio WTI-to-WCS has declined. I think a similar event could occur with the Vaca Muerta Sur Oil Pipeline. If this occurs, Vista Energy will improve its net income margin.

High Profit Margin

Vista has a high profit margin, which is a competitive advantage. To begin with, the company operates in Vaca Muerta, Argentina, a region rich in shale oil and shale gas. Therefore, I think that comparing Vista with some relevant oil and gas producers in the Permian Basin is an interesting analysis. Oil companies in the Permian Basin also extract shale oil.

For implementing the analysis, I chose some of the strongest players in the Permian Basin, which are Occidental Petroleum (OXY), Diamondback Energy (FANG), EOG Resources (EOG), Permian Resources (PR), Ovintiv (OVV), and Matador Resources (MTDR). I believe these companies are among the most profitable companies in the Permian Basin.

With that being said, the following table shows some profitability ratios of Vista and the aforementioned companies. Vista Energy has the highest return on equity, with 32.74%. In addition, it has the highest net income margin with 29.12%. Therefore, Vista is more profitable than some of the most relevant players in the Permian Basin.

PEPASA

In April 2025, Vista Energy announced the acquisition of PEPASA. With this acquisition, Vista holds a 50% working interest in La Amarga Chica. Meanwhile, the remaining 50% belongs to YPF, which is the operator of the concession. Thanks to this purchase, Vista's proved reserves increased by 140MMboe. In addition, it added 39,772 boe/d to its production.

The acquisition will also create value for investors. The following table shows the profitability ratios of Vista and PEPASA in 2024. All the metrics indicate that PEPASA has higher profitability ratios than Vista. For instance, in 2024, PEPASA reported a net profit margin of 38.37%, while Vista reported a 28.98% net income margin.

Oil Price Outlook

To forecast Vista's revenues, it is necessary to project the oil price. Therefore, this section analyzes the WTI forward curve and uses its values to project oil prices.

The forward curve is a valid tool to project oil prices. It shows the future oil prices at which oil producers and refineries are hedging their operations. For instance, if an oil company hedges its oil production at $61 per WTI barrel, it indicates that this price is convenient for the company. As observed in the chart below, WTI oil futures contracts have prices in the range of $60-$63 per barrel. This range could be seen as the long-term WTI oil price, since market participants are buying and selling future contracts at those prices.

Up to this point, the WTI forward curve suggests oil prices in the range of $60-$63 per barrel. Based on this range, I assume that the WTI will average $61 per barrel in 2026 and 2027, as depicted in the table below. Moving to the short-term, from January 1 to August 11 of this year, the WTI averaged $68.06 per barrel, representing an 11.19% decline compared to 2024. I assume that the previous price will be the average price of this commodity for 2025. Finally, I speculate that Vista's realized oil price per barrel will decline in the same proportion as the WTI.

Production Outlook

Oil and gas production is the other component necessary to project Vista's revenues. In the first half of 2023, the company produced 50 Mboe/d and projected to extract 150 Mboe/d by 2030. However, the acquisition of PEPASA boosted its oil production. In fact, in the second half of this year, it expects to produce 125-128 Mboe/d. Consequently, the company is close to reaching its previous goal of 150 Mboe/d. In the Q2 Earnings Transcripts, the company says that it could produce 200 Mboe/d in 2027. This projection assumes that the Vaca Muerta Sur Oil Pipeline will be operational in that year.

For this year, I assume that the production will grow by 62.22%, as depicted in the following table. I took this value from the earnings guidance of the company. Meanwhile, in 2026 and 2027, I assume that the production will grow each year at an annual growth rate of 19.06%. This percentage is the compound annual growth rate (OTC:CAGR) of the production between 2019 and 2024. By 2027, my estimation indicates that the company will produce 160 Mboe/d. This value is lower than the 200 Mboe/d that the company said it could reach in 2027. As such, it is a conservative projection.

Projection of Revenues and Adjusted EBITDA

The previous two sections have the ingredients to project Vista Energy's revenues. The Oil Price Outlook Section projected the WTI oil price, while the Production Outlook Section forecasted Vista's oil production. By combining both projections, we have Vista's revenue growth rate, as depicted in the following table. For instance, in 2025, Vista's revenues are expected to grow at a 51.03% ((-11.19%+ 62.22%)). For 2027, the projection estimates that the company will reach $3,221 million in revenues, a 95.45% increase compared to 2024.

Finally, in Q2 2025, Vista Energy reported a 66.26% adjusted EBITDA margin. I use this value to project the adjusted EBITDA for the upcoming years, as depicted in the following table. My projection suggests that by 2027, Vista's adjusted EBITDA margin will reach $2,134 million, representing a 95.34% increase compared to 2024.

Macroeconomic Risk

Argentina has defaulted on its sovereign bonds on nine occasions. The country is still borrowing financial resources from the IMF. These resources will be used to restore its macroeconomic disequilibrium. However, the country could not implement its stabilization plan successfully. If this occurs again, Vista's operations could be negatively impacted.

Debt Risk

Debt is another risk factor for Vista. There are wonderful businesses with excellent growth outlooks, but they experience financial distress due to their high debt levels. I think this could happen to Vista Energy if it continues to increase its debt levels.

The following table shows the debt-to-equity and current ratios of Vista, OXY, FANG, EOG, PR, OVV, MTDR, and YPF. Vista has a 125.84% debt-to-equity ratio, the highest value among its peers. In addition, it has the lowest current ratio with 0.41. Both figures indicate that Vista has high debt levels. If oil prices decline and remain at low levels for several years, Vista could experience financial distress.

Valuation Ratios

This section compares some valuation ratios of Vista, OXY, FANG, EOG, PR, OVV, MTDR, and YPF. It intends to compare Vista with some relevant oil producers.

As depicted in the table above, Vista has an 8.3 P/E ratio, lower than the median ratio. This ratio suggests that the company is undervalued. However, the company has higher price-to-book and price-to-cash flow ratios than the median of these metrics. Both ratios suggest that Vista is overvalued compared to its peers. Finally, the EV/EBITDA of Vista is similar to the median ratio in the peer group, suggesting that the company is neither undervalued nor overvalued.

To conclude, the valuation ratios point out that the company presents an undervaluation, as evidenced by the price-to-book and price-to-cash flow ratios.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I still maintain my Buy rating for Vista. The company still has a high growth potential. By 2027, my projection suggests that Vista Energy could increase its oil production to 160 thousand barrels of oil equivalent per day ((Mboe/d)). Assuming a 66.26% adjusted EBITDA margin ((the margin reported in Q2 2025)), Vista's adjusted EBITDA would increase by 95.34% compared to 2024.

The company has certain competitive advantages compared to other shale oil producers. Its breakeven price is around $40. Meanwhile, in the Permian Basin, oil companies tend to have higher breakeven prices. Furthermore, the acquisition of PEPASA will improve Vista's profit margin, another positive aspect.

However, the company has significant debt levels. It has a 125.84% debt-to-equity ratio, the highest ratio among some relevant oil fracking companies. If oil prices decline, Vista could experience financial distress due to its high debt levels. Vista's operations are concentrated in Argentina, a country that has experienced several economic crises. As such, the company could be negatively impacted if Argentina faces another crisis. Nonetheless, when taking all of these aspects into consideration, I still consider Vista to be an excellent investment opportunity.

data.title: BNY Mellon New York AMT-Free Municipal Bond Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The municipal bond market experienced elevated volatility relative to Treasuries in April, as a tariff-induced bond selloff coincided with a seasonally weak period for supply and demand. May and June conditions improved as municipal bond performance recovered in line with Treasuries, supported by seasonally strong demand. For the second quarter, municipal bonds overall underperformed other fixed income markets, in part due to record-setting new issue supply and muted mutual fund demand. During 2Q25, total issuance topped $160billion (bn), up 13% compared to 2Q24. Year-to-date taxable municipal issuance held steady at approximately $18bn compared to the first quarters of last year. Demand from retail investors was mixed as municipal open-ended mutual funds experienced $2.6bn in net outflows (per Investment Company Institute (ICI)) compared to ETFs which attracted $8.8bn of inflows during 2Q25.

Performance Summary

For the quarter ended June 30, 2025, the Fund’s Class I shares returned -0.86%, excluding sales charges. In comparison, the Fund’s unmanaged benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Municipal Bond Index, returned -1.22% for the same period.

During the second quarter, the AAA municipal yield curve continued to steepen as short-term yields decreased, while intermediate and longer maturity bond yields increased. Municipal AAA yields decreased 5 to 15 basis points (bps) for two-to-five maturities, while yields increased by 1 to 26 bps for ten- to thirty-year maturities. The 2y and 30y AAA municipal curve steepened 31bps in 2Q25 to 188bps. Similarly, US treasury curve steepened, with yields from two- to five-year maturities decreasing 13 to 16 bps, while yields from ten-to-thirty years increased 6 to 25bps. The Muni-Treasury ratio, a key gauge of relative value, lost value slightly during 2Q25 across all maturity tenors. The 10Y AAA Muni-to-Treasury ratio increased slightly to 76%, while the 30Y ratio cheapened slightly to 95% from 92%, still looking inexpensive compared to historical levels from the past few years. During the recent quarter, longer duration municipal bonds underperformed their shorter counterparts, reflected in the Bloomberg Municipal Long Index return of -1.94%, compared to the Bloomberg Municipal 3-year and 10-year Index returns of +1.05% and +0.78%, respectively.

From a quality perspective during 2Q25, the lower rated segments delivered relatively weaker performance, reflected by the Bloomberg Municipal BBB & A Index return of -0.64% and -0.24%, respectively, underperforming the Bloomberg Municipal AAA & AA Index returns of -0.09% and -0.04%, respectively. From a sector perspective, the revenue sector underperformed general obligation (GO) bonds. Tobacco, housing, and industrials sub-sectors remained to be the outperformers during the quarter, while hospital, education, and water and sewer sub-sectors were generally weaker performers. Credit spreads continued to widen during 2Q25 as rate volatility, macro-economic, and tax policy uncertainty was heightened. The Bloomberg Municipal High-yield Index returned -1.14% during 2Q25, underperforming the investment-grade Bloomberg Municipal index, which returned -0.12%. The Bloomberg Taxable Municipal Index returned +0.81% during the second quarter, compared to the Bloomberg US Treasury Index return of +0.85% and the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Index return of +1.82%.

We believe municipal credit conditions remain solid, supported by very high reserves and cash balances that have accumulated over the last few years. A number of executive actions taken by the Trump Administration, if allowed to stand, could have negative credit implications across large segments of public finance. Tariffs, federal job cuts, and the mass deportation of illegal immigrants are likely to slow economic growth, which could have a negative impact on income and sales tax receipts. At the same time, cuts in federal spending in areas such as Medicaid, “green” infrastructure initiatives, and federal disaster relief could pressure state/local budgets.

The US economy has moved into a ‘falling’ growth regime led by recent tariff policy uncertainty which should drive a softer economic backdrop for 2H25. Inflation data over recent months has re-affirmed the slow, but gradual disinflation regime that we have been in for several years. We expect the Fed to remain patient and resume easing by year-end in response to moderating growth and a weaker labor market. Muni yields should follow Treasuries with the prospect of a further steepening yield curve led by the pull of expected Fed rate cuts on the short-end.

Performance Review

The Fund’s long-duration profile and exposure to longer-term maturities detracted as yields in that part of the curve rose for the quarter. Contributions from security and sector allocation was negligible for the period.

Market Outlook

Elevated financial market volatility is likely to continue amid ongoing uncertainty around tariffs, geopolitical alliances, and overall policy change prospects tied to federal funding, among others. We remain vigilant with focus on well diversified, high-quality portfolios consisting of solid fundamental credits that we believe are well-positioned to withstand pressures from potential policy changes in Washington. We also would view any market dislocation stemming from federal policy uncertainty as a potential buying opportunity.

Uncertain changes to federal policy could drive heightened volatility in credit conditions as we move through 2025. We believe a more cautious approach to credit selection would be prudent, with an increased emphasis on balance sheet strength, liquidity and operating flexibility. Essential service sectors, including public power and water/sewer, remain relatively sound investments due to their predictable and stable cash flows through economic cycles. Healthcare is facing potential headwinds stemming from potential cuts to Medicaid, which could have an outsized negative impact on smaller, rural hospitals. Threatened cuts to federal grants and potential changes to endowment tax rates are driving negative headlines among many of the higher rated colleges and universities, yet we believe these institutions have substantial resources to withstand the cuts and would view any spread widening as a buying opportunity. We continue to see value in the transportation sectors although airports with heavy reliance on international travel could see some softening in demand.

Our duration posture is targeted neutral to modestly long versus respective benchmarks. The recent backup in municipal yields along with yield curve steepening has made the current environment more attractive for modest duration extension. The prospect for transitory impacts of higher inflation from fiscal and tariff policy from the new administration along with the Fed’s dovish bias may keep the slope of the curve elevated and rangebound in the near term.

Read the prospectus carefully before investing. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Risks

Bonds are subject generally to interest-rate, credit, liquidity, call, and market risks, to varying degrees. Generally, all other factors being equal, bond prices are inversely related to interest-rate changes and rate increases can cause price declines. Municipal income may be subject to state and local taxes. Some income may be subject to the federal alternative minimum tax for certain investors. Capital gains, if any, are taxable. The amount of public information available about municipal securities is generally less than that for corporate equities or bonds. Legislative changes, state and local economic and business developments, may adversely affect the yield and/or value of municipal securities. Other factors include the general conditions of the municipal securities market, the size of the particular offering, maturity of the obligation, and the rating of the issue. Asset allocation and diversification cannot ensure a profit or protect against loss in declining markets.

The Bloomberg US Municipal Bond Index is a widely accepted, unmanaged total return performance benchmark for the long-term, investment-grade, tax-exempt bond market. Reflects investments of dividends and, where applicable, capital gain distributions. The Bloomberg US Treasury Index measures US dollar-denominated, fixed-rate, nominal debt issued by the US Treasury. Treasury bills are excluded by the maturity constraint but are part of a separate Short Treasury Index. The Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Index measures the performance of the investment-grade, fixed-rate, taxable corporate bond market within the United States. The Bloomberg Municipal Managed Money Long Index is a rules-based, market-value-weighted index that measures the performance of publicly traded, USD-denominated, long-term, tax-exempt municipal bonds, including general obligation bonds, revenue bonds, insured bonds, and pre-refunded bonds. The Bloomberg 3-Year Municipal Bond Index is a benchmark that tracks the performance of a specific segment of the municipal bond market. It includes fixed-rate, investment-grade, tax-exempt bonds with remaining maturities between two and four years. The Bloomberg 1-10 Year Municipal Bond Index tracks the performance of investment-grade municipal bonds with maturities between 1 and 10 years. Bloomberg Municipal Bond AAA, AA, A and BBB Indices are subsets of the Bloomberg Barclays Municipal Bond Index. The Bloomberg Municipal High Yield Index is a benchmark that tracks the performance of the non-investment grade, tax-exempt municipal bond market in the United States. It includes bonds that are rated below investment grade (Ba1 or lower) or are unrated and are U.S. dollar-denominated and fixed-rate. The Bloomberg Municipal Taxable Bonds Index measures the performance of the U.S. municipal taxable investment-grade bond market. It includes bonds issued by state and local governments that are subject to federal income tax. Investors cannot invest directly in any index.

A yield curve is a line that plots the yields or interest rates of bonds that have equal credit quality but different maturity dates. The slope of the yield curve predicts the direction of interest rates and the economic expansion or contraction that could result.

Duration is a measure of the sensitivity of the price of a bond or other debt instrument to a change in interest rates. A bond's duration is easily confused with its term or time to maturity because they are both measured in years. However, a bond's term is a linear measure of the years until repayment of principal is due; it does not change with the interest rate environment. Duration, on the other hand is non-linear and accelerates as time to maturity lessens. Dovish describes a monetary policy stance that favors lower interest rates to stimulate economic growth and potentially increase employment, even if it means accepting a higher level of inflation. The Muni/Treasury ratio is a measure that compares the yield of municipal bonds to that of U.S. Treasury bonds. It's a key metric for assessing the relative value of municipal bonds, particularly for investors considering the tax benefits of munis.

data.title: A-Mark Precious Metals: Interest In The Stock And Products Has Waned
data.description:
I love getting to know stocks before broader discovery, though the ensuing period of obscurity can be tough to endure. One of my long-time favorites has been A-Mark Precious Metals (NASDAQ:AMRK), which I first wrote up on Seeking Alpha in 2019. The piece got a single comment (from myself), and the stock has returned over 600% since, even after the recent weakness. I’ve been less vocal on it in the past year, as interest in the company significantly increased. But since then, A-Mark has underperformed in an environment that, on the surface, might seem favorable for a precious metals distributor. The lackluster stock performance can be traced to three primary factors: compression in product premiums, declining demand from key demographic groups, and the counterintuitive impact of higher gold and silver prices on AMRK’s business model.

Premium Compression

The business historically thrives during periods of heightened market anxiety, when premiums for physical gold and silver products spike due to supply shortages, retail panic, or a surge in new buyers. In the years following the onset of Covid-19, for example, A-Mark benefited from extraordinary spreads. Over the past year, premiums on bullion coins and bars, particularly U.S. Silver Eagles, have dropped sharply from the extreme highs to historical averages.

This premium compression has had direct effects on their profitability. While elevated spot metal prices might suggest potential for higher profits, A-Mark’s profitability relies far more on the spread between wholesale acquisition and retail sale prices than on spot prices themselves. As premiums narrowed, A-Mark’s gross profit margins on transactions fell from over 3% in FY22 down to about 1.3% in the most recent quarters, a very material drop for a low-margin, high-turnover business, which rising revenues have not been sufficient to offset.

Declining Demand from Core Demographics

A-Mark’s business is shaped by the purchasing patterns of a distinct set of buyers who are usually triggered by economic or geopolitical shocks and safe haven investors who are particularly sensitive to inflation and political news cycles. Historically, these demographics have generally been more active metals buyers under Democratic administrations than Republican. Historically, Glenn Beck has been a spokesman for A-Mark brands, if you're looking for some evidence of this correlation.

In 2024, market volatility significantly declined from recent highs, giving retail investors less urgency to shift assets into safe havens like bullion. Many “silver stackers” and gold buyers from the second group, burned by price volatility or holding expensive inventory from the last cycle's highs, became net sellers or inactive, shrinking A-Mark’s retail-facing volumes. And of course, Donald Trump was re-elected as President.

A-Mark has made efforts to expand its international business and target new customer pools, but these are still a nascent part of the revenue base. I had previously hoped that A-Mark's Cybermetals offering would draw in more of the cryptocurrency crowd to their products, but the ongoing contributions from the offering remain immaterial.

Why Haven’t Metal Prices Helped AMRK?

At first glance, many have assumed that higher spot prices for gold and silver would benefit a major precious metals distributor. But for A-Mark, whose model is built on hedged inventory, this isn’t the case.

A-Mark’s core business is structured to eliminate price exposure; virtually all inventory is hedged (see above), so its net results are driven by transaction spreads and inventory velocity, not appreciation in underlying metals. As spot prices rose and premiums fell, smaller dealers have flooded the wholesale market with unhedged inventory they'd accumulated at lower prices, leading to oversupply and lower A-Mark market share. Higher metals prices and lower premiums also directly correlate to lower margins, as discussed above.

What’s more, rising metals prices present a unique challenge to A-Mark's business model. They finance their inventory via short-term loans, which price at low rates due to their backing by liquid gold and silver products. But as prices rise, A-Mark requires more working capital to maintain similar inventory positions. They can own half as much silver at $20 as at $40, without taking on incremental debt. As a result, steadily building inventory has challenged A-Mark's cash generation and increased the interest drag on earnings.

Management Strategy and Acquisitions

Despite these headwinds, A-Mark continues to pursue tuck-in acquisitions, seeking to expand its offering by absorbing additional DTC brands and investing in expanded logistics capabilities. A-Mark's M&A approach has generally involved taking minority stakes in related businesses and slowly increasing their ownership interest. They ran this playbook to perfection with JMBullion, and most recently with Pinehurst.

On a less-favorable note, I was not a fan of their recent consolidation of Spectrum Group (SGB), which had previously spun out the A-Mark entity in 2013. The deal was done at a significant premium to a tender offer at Spectrum just a few months prior and has had a lot of current A-Mark executives at both companies during their time apart. I do appreciate cleaning up related-party issues, but this one didn't give me warm feelings.

Market Sentiment and Valuation

Management recognizes the cyclicality of the business and will cite historical precedent for rebound performance during geopolitical or economic distress, but current supply-demand dynamics and a lack of fear-driven buyer interest suggest a slow return to outsized profitability.

After trading as high as $47 in September 2024, AMRK’s stock has fallen over 50%, reflecting the reality that investors prize high, stable margins and earnings growth. Investors are usually unwilling to pay a premium multiple for a highly cyclical, low-margin business facing unfavorable supply-demand conditions, even when net income is positive and the company’s balance sheet remains strong.

When I won The Investor’s Podcast’s 2022 pitch competition with this stock, I pointed to long-term sustainable EBITDA around $100m and $75m of FCF in normalized environments, and while TTM EBITDA has dipped closer to $70m, I still think this is a reasonable long-term expectation for the business. As I noted above, interest expense has expanded with higher metals prices, and this will hamper cash conversion for the time being. But if prices do drop in the future, the working capital windfall for A-Mark should be tremendous.

Conclusion

AMRK is a spreads and flows business, not a direct play on metal prices. Premium compression, a complacent customer base, and the paradoxical impact of rising metal prices appear to have conspired to suppress earnings and compress valuation multiples over the past year. While the company remains well managed, nimble, and positioned for any resurgence in global panic or instability, absent another period of extraordinary retail demand, margin compression and soft DTC volumes will likely remain headwinds for the foreseeable future.

data.title: RE/MAX Survives By Doing The Right Things, Share Buybacks Could Be Next
data.description:
Investment Thesis

On June 10th, I wrote an article about RE/MAX (RMAX) called “A Traditional Player That's Still Worth It”, and I tried to show the virtues of the company (such as its solid agent network and stable revenues) and its experience in the sector given its extensive experience operating in the real estate market.

I have also focused on the difficult situation of the housing sector, and in the competition from real estate tech firms, and although I still think that those factors justify a subvaluation, I'm sure this is a little bit too harsh, and that's why I rated the company as "Buy", because if it maintains its solid performance, keeps its debt under control, and manages to stabilize the decline in its agent count, the possibility of improvement in a more favorable context (with lower rates and more real estate buying), is real and concrete, and sooner or later, it will be reflected in the stock price, which has remained at historical lows since 2024.

In fact, since my article, the total return has been 10.3%, beating the SPY's 6.4% gain, despite 2Q2025 results that weren't very good (I will analyze the quarter later). Even so, the shares had a little rebound.

On this new article, I would like to add 2 key factors that I haven't mentioned in my last article, and that I consider are very important to keep on investing in RMAX for the long term: the possibility that the company re-buys shares, and resuming dividend payments (it seems to be a complex alternative but not impossible).

Share Buybacks

Share repurchases are a concrete possibility, the directors informed on the Q2 report, although to do it, the company is forced to reduce its TLR (Total Liquidity Ratio) less than 3.5x over the last 12 months, a restriction of its Senior Secured Credit Facility. About the possibility of repurchasing the shares, they said:

Share Repurchases and Retirement

As previously disclosed, in January 2022 the Company's Board of Directors authorized a common stock repurchase program of up to $100 million. During the three months ended June 30, 2025, the Company did not repurchase any shares. As of June 30, 2025, $62.5 million remained available under the share repurchase program.

For the company to make a repurchase of $62.5 million, as I mentioned before, it must reduce its leverage less than 3.5x, and on July 30th it is on 3.58x, a slight decrease if we compare it to the 3.61x of 1Q2025. This requirement is described on slide 17 of the 10-Q. The TLR nearly reached the decrease in 3Q2024, hitting 3.52x, but then climbed again, before showing a new decline in the most recently presented quarter.

If the company achieves another strong decline over this year, it is probable that we'll see a great capital liberation that will increase the EPS, which is expected to continue on $1.29 (which implies a stagnation regarding 2024 and 2023). That's why it could change the scenario of repurchasing, and the calculation is relatively simple: with fewer shares outstanding and keeping stable the net income and the P/E (that today is very undervalued), the gain is immediate. To evaluate this margin, I prepared a chart with five possible scenarios, starting with a conservative $5 million repurchase of the $62.5 million available, until a more ambitious $25 million buyback.

Assuming the most conservative scenarios are the most realistic, the instant upside is roughly between 8 and 18%. I also don't think it's unreasonable to consider a stronger buyback scenario, and in that case, the upside soars to 64%. I want to clarify that I'm being realistic and conservative to illustrate the opportunity RMAX presents in this regard, and I'm assuming they'll use less than 50% of available capital for buybacks. But even so, there's another attractive opportunity, though less likely, in my opinion: dividends.

Dividends, A Possibility That Still Requires Better Cash Flow

I'll analyze in a little more detail the medium-term possibilities regarding the company's dividends, as RMAX has never been known as a star dividend company and, in my opinion, can never be compared to REITs that offer consistent dividends, but rather to similar real estate franchises. Until 2023, the company maintained a regular payout for almost 10 years, with the last average annual payout (made in August 2023) of $0.64, yielding approximately 3.6% and with the stock priced at around $19.

In a hypothetical return to dividend payments, Seeking Alpha's Dividends section reflects possible scenarios based on an EPS of $1.29, and the option to return to a payout similar to that of 2023 would have to be within a Payout Ratio of between 40 and 60%, which would leave a yield a bit high considering the company's historical record.

But in the current scenario, there are clearly other variables at play that have to do with cash flow and revenue generation that, although stable, has clearly been impacted by a bear market in recent years. Free cash flow, for example, closed the first half of 2025 at $9.8 million, a drop of almost 39% compared to $16.8 million in the same period of the previous year, which represents a limitation for opening a new front of fixed expenses such as dividend payments. At the close of 2024, the FCF position was more attractive at $51 million, but 2025 is proving to be a challenging year for RMAX.

Cash flow is an obvious constraint on dividend payments, but not on share repurchases. I don't rule out the possibility that RMAX, in a more favorable operating and market environment, will return to dividends, but I believe there is a much clearer likelihood of share repurchases.

2Q2025: Surviving a Bearish Environment

The 2Q2025 results left a downward adjustment outlook for the full year on total revenue from $310 million to $296 million at the high end, and the same was true for adjusted EBITDA, where the estimate fell from $100 million to $95 million at the high end, demonstrating the greater challenges the company is facing. In that regard, CEO Erik Carlson commented the following on the conference call:

Uncertainty around tariffs, inflation and consumer confidence, coupled with affordability challenges, including persistently high mortgage rates have caused us to temper our expectations around a potential housing rebound in the latter half of the year.

For now, we remain laser-focused, focused on the things we can control, and our Q2 results are a testament to our efforts and to the resilience of our network and team.

Beyond the difficulties, I believe it's important to highlight the stability of net income, which was $4.7 million versus $3.7 million in 2Q2024. This stability is highlighted by the fact that total revenue fell 7% year-over-year to $72 million. Added to this is an EPS that closed at $0.39, $0.04 above projections, while the adjusted EBITDA Margin rose 30 basis points, from 35.8% to 36.1%, a demonstration, in my opinion, of how the company operates efficiently by managing costs and adapting to market circumstances. From this, we conclude that it is surviving well, despite losing nearly 4,000 agents in the United States and Canada, where it generates nearly 76% of its revenue. In the total count, agents are growing due to the momentum in markets outside the United States and Canada; nearly 3,500 more agents were added in the last 12 months. However, when translated into income, this figure does not compensate.

That's why RMAX is trying to innovate to attract new agents with new tools, such as the Aspire program, launched last April to reduce some of the fixed costs new agents must pay, representing a more attractive model compared to the company's traditional recruiting model. Initially, Aspire would have affected revenue in the second quarter, as CFO Karri Callahan explained in the conference call, and could put additional pressure on earnings in the second half. She explained it as follows:

So Aspire has really been a fantastic, we've seen fantastic results from a business perspective and from an adoption standpoint as it really accelerated recruiting. April, May and June were the best months of recruiting we've seen in the U.S. But from a revenue perspective, near term, it's going to take a little while for us to see the revenue contribution. At scale, we think that the revenue per agent is on par or even a little bit better than what we see today. But on a near-term basis in the back half of the year, there's a little bit of pressure as those things ramp up.

The quick takeaway is that it's a young program, and according to both Callahan and Carlson, it's yielding good results, but initially it will represent less revenue due to lower commissions. Consider this an interesting venture and one that will be worth monitoring closely.

Valuation

Regarding valuation, I believe the multiples are clear and evident, easily observable in the Valuation section of Seeking Alpha. As I explained previously, I assume many of these multiples reflect the company's decline in agents and the difficult environment in the sector.

But I want to briefly focus on the fact that an improvement in the sector's sentiment, driven by a decrease in interest rates (a likely scenario for the end of the year or early 2026), a drop in mortgage rates, or a rebound in home purchases, will mean that if RMAX continues to do things well, in my opinion, it will be easier for it to increase its revenue.

To calculate favorable scenarios, I took the current stock price of around $8.50, used the aforementioned EPS of $1.29, and applied increases in the FWD P/E in the following table, assuming any of the aforementioned variables increase the multiple.

Scenario P/E FWD Target Price Upside
Pessimistic 6x $7.74 -8.9%
Conservative 8x $10.32 +21.4%
Optimistic 10x $12.90 +51.8%
Bullish 12x $15.48 +82.1%

The most optimistic scenario shows a potential of over 80%, but if we stay between a FWD P/E of 8x and 10x, the upside could move between 20 and 50%, a possibility I believe to be more reasonable.

Risks And Competition

The risks remain the same as those I described a couple of months ago in my previous article, where the pessimistic housing market represents the biggest obstacle to the company's development. In this case, I add cash generation, a key factor in sustaining future share buybacks and financial strength, which must therefore be closely monitored.

There is another issue, also gaining importance among the risks and which I also mentioned in the previous article, which has to do with technological competition, and which I now want to give more attention to.

There is intense competition in the sector that is reducing internal costs with proprietary software and proposals that reduce intermediation. Among these cases are Compass (COMP) and The Real Brokerage (REAX) as the most efficient tech companies, and then there is another group of real estate tech companies that I consider somewhat more volatile and not as reliable in terms of efficiency, such as Opendoor Technologies (OPEN), Zillow (Z), or eXp (EXPI). RMAX is boosting its operations with its Mortgage network, which already represents 10% of the company's revenue, and also promises to recover agents and markets with Aspire, as I mentioned earlier.

Conclusion

RMAX, in my opinion, is overly battered by the bearish real estate market and the weak performance of home sales. However, its financial profile is still solid, with manageable debt, still positive cash generation, and a concrete opportunity for upside potential from a share buyback already announced by the board of directors.

If the sector doesn't recover, but the company continues to reduce its leverage and bring its TLR below 3.5x, the potential remains a real possibility. If the sector recovers, the upside could be even greater. We are still in a very bearish moment, which I still consider ideal for buying.

data.title: Why Exxon Mobil's Strong Fundamentals And Technical Setup Make It A Compelling Buy
data.description:
Over the past four years, Exxon Mobil Corporation (NYSE:XOM) has proven exceptionally resilient, racking up an impressive 300%+ revaluation since reaching its October 2020 lows of $30.11 to the recent high of $126.34. Trading within a critical technical triangle formation near all-time channel highs, the oil and gas major is now at an inflection point in which solid operational fundamentals meet strong technical catalysts. With a forward P/E of only 16.17x, a solid 3.69% dividend yield, and recent earnings beats confirming strong operational excellence, Exxon offers a compelling risk-reward proposition for investors looking for exposure to a recovery in the energy sector.

In my view, Exxon has demonstrated it can traverse the energy transition while maintaining the highest profitability metrics in the industry. Exxon's most recent results for Q2 2025 confirmed continued earnings power demonstrated by normalized EPS of $1.64 beating consensus by $0.08 and revenue of $81.51 billion exceeding expectations by > $1 billion. The technical set-up suggests Exxon's stock could have a breakout into the $170 area, representing approximately 55% upside from the current price levels. Taken together with Seeking Alpha Quant's Strong Buy rating and Exxon's A+ profitability grade, I see an attractive Buy recommendation for Exxon's equity while generating income and capital appreciation in the energy sector.

Business Update: Operational Excellence Driving Consistent Results

Exxon Mobil put out excellent Q2 2025 results that validated the company's operating and capital efficiency, which supports Exxon as an industry leader. The company reported normalized earnings per share of $1.64 that beat consensus estimates by $0.08 and demonstrated the advantages of its integrated model. The company's revenue reached $81.51 billion, which also beat estimates by $1.01 billion, again highlighting the significant headwinds of volatile commodity prices and uncertain global economic conditions.

The company's diversified operations that include upstream exploration and production, downstream refining, and chemical manufacturing represent natural hedges to commodity price volatility. In upstream operations, strength from the Permian Basin continues to benefit the company with ongoing operational benefits from the transformative Pioneer Natural Resources acquisition starting in 2024, which expanded Exxon’s heels and expanded to a world-class position in one of North America’s most productive oil basins. The strategic acquisition is already benefiting Exxon through current synergies and efficiencies expected to increase through 2025 and beyond.

The downstream side was able to keep high margins, benefiting from global refining capacity limitations. The Energy Products segment continued to be buoyed by high demand in transportation fuels and petrochemicals, while the integrated hydrocarbon value chain through its operations was also able to generate additional margins. Chemical products, with a large market for olefins and polyolefins softer in volume, provided strength, with emerging markets continuing to provide petrochemical imports with industrialization robust.

In respect to guidance, management shared a positive expected outlook for Q3 2025 based on normalized EPS estimates of $1.71, and revenue estimates of $85.32 billion. The company continues to allocate its capital towards high-return low-cost projects, which allows them to provide a sustainable return by generating consistent cash flows even if commodity prices head lower, and continues to commit to maintaining its capital discipline as a sustainable involved.

Technical Analysis: Triangle Formation Points to Significant Breakout Potential

From a purely technical viewpoint, Exxon Mobil has one of the strongest setups in the energy sector. The stock has been on a solid uptrend after a large double bottom in October of 2020, with its price moving from $30.11 to a recent all-time high of $126.34—over 300% appreciation in four years. This long-term uptrend is intact and has been definitively characterized by higher highs and higher lows, which is clear evidence of institutional accumulation.

In looking at the current technical structure, Exxon is trading in two parallel channels that over the last nearly twenty years have provided resistance and support. The stock has recently traded at the maximum upper channel of it's long-term formation and could continue its upward momentum above this level. Additionally, following the recent all-time highs, a shorter-term triangle pattern has formed, resulting in a coiling pattern that typically results in uncoiling or sharp moves in either direction.

The triangle remains unresolved, with neither the upper or lower trendline cleanly penetrated. However, with several technical indicators pointing to an upward resolution, an upside resolution is more likely than a downside. The descending trendline above, while longer (and maybe more important), is being measured against the ascending trendline below that is accumulating pressure from below. The stock is situated in the bottom portion of the long-term channel, with an overall bullish structure, but given that the stock has more "room" to increase than decrease, it seems the breakup is more probable.

If the triangle resolves to the upside, the measured move anticipates a target of $170, which would represent around 55% upside from current levels. This target corresponds with the upper end of the long-term channel, providing additional technical confirmation. If it breaks down, expect to target the area around $98, which corresponds with important technical support and the downside projection of the triangle.

The moving average structure on the weekly chart provides more context. The stock is above the critical 200-week moving average. The 50-week and 100-week averages are currently acting as resistance, suggesting a consolidation period. The daily time frame is moving laterally in line with the triangle structure. The market is clearly building energy for the next move.

Meanwhile, the stochastic oscillator is almost oversold, which tends to coincide historically with momentum reversals to the upside. The technical evidence implies the stock may be gathering momentum to break upwards. That said, it is very important to keep in mind that momentum indicators can remain extreme for extended time periods when in a strong trending market.

Shareholder Returns Excellence: Unprecedented Capital Return Program

Exxon Mobil has demonstrated it is the king of shareholder returns in the energy industry and has embarked on one of the most robust and well-capitalized capital return programs in corporate America. Exxon committed to the total capital return of $20 billion annually through share repurchases in 2025 and 2026, with cash flow of $165 billion in surplus cash flow likely through 2030. This expansive capital return program is a signal of management's optimism about the business and its determination to maximize shareholder value.

Exxon's share repurchase program is already working well; Exxon has purchased back about 40% of the shares it issued to acquire Pioneer Natural Resources since the acquisition closed in May 2024. This constant decline in share count improves earnings per share growth and is a testament to management's commitment to value and the responsible allocation of capital. Exxon is on target to repurchase $20 billion in shares in the rest of 2025, which would be one of the largest share repurchase plans in the market.

Apart from share repurchase, Exxon boasts an extremely favorable dividend history in the S&P 500, having raised its dividend for 42 years—an impressive feat that only 4% of index members can also claim. Even with the rising costs of capital expenditure and working itself back up to prior levels, the company is yielding 3.69%, making it a compelling income-generating investment in large part due to the cushioning ability of Exxon's incredible cash flow generation to continue to drive dividend increases. Historically, Exxon's trend has been to increase their dividend annually by roughly 6%, and management is hopeful that this trend will continue through at least 2030.

Exxon's returns are predicated on the fact that they have a low-cost asset base achieved through its operational efficiency and synergies across areas. If oil prices are maintained above $65 per barrel, management believes it can provide enough cash flow surplus to cover both dividends and timing commitments for share repurchase while exhibiting capital discipline. At present, commodities are way above the $65 price point, providing anybody who owns the stock ample cushion.

This combination of dependable dividends and aggressive share repurchase programs creates numerous paths for value-add for shareholders: current income while also having future appreciation potential, which should help distinguish Exxon from purely growth-type energy investments.

Valuation: Attractive Entry Point Despite Recent Outperformance

Despite its solid operational performance and technical momentum, Exxon Mobil is still trading at the comparable attractive valuation metrics relative to both its historical levels and growth outlook. The current forward P/E of 16.17x represents a modest premium to the sector median of 11.90x; however, the price is reasonable given Exxon's profitability metrics and operational scale. Compared to Exxon's five year average P/E of 12.71x, the current multiple implies some operational performance improvement and investor confidence in Exxon's strategic direction.

The enterprise value to sales of 1.49x is actually trading at a discount to the sector median of 1.88x; this dynamic and evidence of continued operational improvements and strategic positioning suggest the market has not fully recognized Exxon's position within the context of the industry.

The enterprise value to EBITDA of 7.85x provides another metric, and context, for valuation; Exxon's ratio is trading slightly above the sector median of 6.42x, yet below Exxon's five-year average of 9.02x. The stock appears to have fairly corresponding valuations to cash flow generation and would likely be able to support multiple expansions as operating improvements compound.

With a price-to-book ratio of 1.74x at a premium of the median sector level of 1.28x, it demonstrates the quality of Exxon's asset base and strategic advantages. Nevertheless, this ratio continues to show mostly negative growth against the company's five-year average of 1.90x, indicating there is further expansion potential for valuation as book value rises with retention of earnings and strategic investments.

Confirmation by the Seeking Alpha Quant indicates a balanced valuation with Exxon rated Strong Buy as an investment along with the company's exceptional A+ profitability grade and positive earnings revisions allocated an A- grade from Seeking Alpha. The quantitative framework automatically recognizes operational quality and pricing; the continued upward analyst revisions (20 recommend upward revisions against only three downward revisions over three months) are reinforcing improvement to the price-to-earnings multiple for Exxon's stock price placement. Also, while Seeking Alpha indicated valuation to be D+, which one could view as necessarily high premium pricing, with Exxon's business results and studied analyst updates, the combined improvement of historical profitability indicators with sustained positive ratings gives credence to the overall investment narrative.

Competitive Position: Integrated Model Provides Sustainable Advantages

Exxon Mobil's competitive position within the integrated oil and gas industry includes several structural advantages that make it different from both pure-play exploration and production companies and smaller integrated competitors, including new entrants. Along with the sync of upstream, midstream, and downstream operations, Exxon's global scale with operations in all of the major oil and gas provinces presents natural diversification advantages and reduces exposure to any one single market or regulatory regime.

The integrated business model provides natural hedges across commodity cycles. For example, during periods of declines in upstream oil prices, Exxon may benefit from downstream refining and chemical operations. Integration provides substantially stable cash flow streams as compared to pure upstream companies such as ConocoPhillips or EOG Resources. Exxon's technology and operational scale will provide a tangible advantage over smaller integrated competitors.

In a comparative marketplaces with its primary competitors Chevron Corporation and Shell plc, Exxon's market cap of $458 billion is substantially larger than both Chevron's market cap of $308 billion and Shell's market cap of $210 billion. It is indicative of investors expectations about Exxon's expected operational efficacy and execution of strategy.

Company Forward P/E Forward EV/EBITDA Price/Sales (TTM)
ExxonMobil 16.17x 7.08x 1.45x
Chevron 19.59x 8.25x 1.42x
Shell 11.41x 4.64x 0.80x
Sector Average 11.73x 5.79x 1.23x

Exxon beat sector medians in employee productivity measures, such as net income per employee of $508,000. This affirms favorable productivity through improvements made possible with process improvements from increased productivity with technological advancements.

The recently announced acquisition of Pioneer Natural Resources strengthens Exxon's position in the Permian Basin, one of the lowest oil cost provinces in the world. The acquisition will add approximately 850,000 net acres of top-tier highly prospective acreage providing a substantial inventory opportunity to support long-term production expansion. The strategic value of this acquisition does not only lie in acquiring such acreage. Exxon also bought operational capability and technology solutions that can advance Exxon's unconventional development program.

Exxon has also continually made investments in lower-emission technologies, including carbon capture storage-enabled projects that will position it well for the energy transition while preserving its core competitive advantages by being positioned for the energy transition. These investments will cost a considerable amount of capital but will create potentially new revenue streams while showcasing positive environmental stewardship that continues to impact investors' and stakeholders’ views.

Risk Factors: Commodity Exposure and Transition Challenges

There are a few major risks to consider when assessing Exxon Mobil's investment appeal, although most are not specific to this company - they relate to the industry in general. Commodity price volatility is the key risk; oil and natural gas prices are heavily influenced by geopolitical events, national economic circumstances, and supply-demand balance (or lack of balance); these factors can all materially affect financial performance.

Along these same lines, the global energy transition to renewables creates demand uncertainty for fossil fuels as the future is unknown; however, this transition is going to take decades, not years. Exxon is operating under an integrated model and is investing in lower emissions technologies, which should provide some mitigation, but long-term pressure from policy and advances in technology could cause greater demand destruction of traditional hydrocarbons.

The risk profile of regulation and environment are changing, including the threat of some type of carbon pricing, emissions regulations, and permitting barriers that limit operational flexibility and where capital expenditures can be allocated. Exxon has spent a significant amount of money on compliance to regulator and environmental standards, and investing in lower-emission technologies, which reduces risk profile under these circumstances, but regulators can impose costs and constraints.

Geopolitical risks can affect operations and commodity markets. Given Exxon's global footprint and scattered projects, the prospect of political instability, sanctions, and regulatory flips creates enormous risk for Exxon. Overall, recent geopolitical tensions have generally provided a positive support to energy prices for now, but any new foreign conflict or new government energy/culture policy could emerge and create operational disruptions and volatility in commodity markets.

Investors must be wary of capital allocation risk, as management constantly deliberates on the appropriate balance of growth investments, debt paydown, and returning capital to shareholders. Recent years, the company has been more disciplined with its capital deployment; however, given the commodity price volatility landscape, all it takes is to get caught in extreme market conditions for management response to lead an unfavorable allocation of capital.

Technical risks are present such as a triangle breakdown with $98 support targeted that would signal the potential for weakness as a further breakdown in price. But the technical structure appears sound overall and if we were to see a triangle breakdown, I would suspect it would provide prudent long-term purchasing opportunities.

Convergence of Technical and Fundamental Catalysts

The investment thesis for Exxon Mobil relies on the confluence of several variables that produce a compelling risk-reward tradeoff for investors that want exposure to recovery in the energy sector. The technical environment suggests upside toward $170, while fundamental analysis supports continued cash flows and return to shareholders that will create value over the long term.

From a technical standpoint, the triangle formation and long term channel analysis point to considerable upside as the stock rockets out of consolidation. The revaluation since 2020 demonstrates the stock's capacity to deliver substantial returns in favorable environments and the current setup suggests another major move is imminent.

Fundamentally, Exxon's operational excellence and financial discipline will allow it to outperform both commodity recovery and adverse environments. Integrated business, nature hedging due to diversification of assets, ability to leverage either size or technological attributes to create sustainable competitive advantages.

The sustainability of dividends at current levels offers downside protection while generating attractive income for investors. The current yield and capital appreciation provides several return pathways that buttress the overall investment thesis.

From a valuation perspective, the stock remains reasonably valued in light of a recent period of outperformance, with the potential for multiple expansion as operational improvements continue to compound. The transformative Pioneer acquisition and industry-leading program to return value to shareholders provide a further catalyst for value creation.

Conclusion

Exxon Mobil Corporation offers a unique investment with technical momentum combined with fundamental strength in an energy sector in the midst of a resettlement. As evidenced by its 300% revaluation since 2020, the company brings investors meaningful returns, and with the current triangle it looks like another major move is possibly on the way. With technical targets approaching $170 coupled with fundamental work showing sustainable cash generation, the stock is a Buy.

The combination of operational efficiency, financial discipline, and technical momentum has paved the way for shareholders to receive value. Recent earnings beats along with upward revisions from analysts show that operational improvement has successfully driven up earnings. Also, the 3.69% dividend really helps support our return during consolidation. Furthermore, the transformative Pioneer acquisition combined with the first ever $20 billion buyback program annually provides more catalysts for value.

Despite the energy sector risks of commodity price fluctuations, and longer-term transition drawbacks, Exxon's integrated model and strategic position provide natural hedges and comparative advantages. The fact that the company has made investments into lower-emission technologies shows that it can adapt to changing market conditions while still being 'best-in-class' at its core.

So, for investors looking for potential income and capital appreciation investment opportunities with exposure to energy sector recovery, Exxon Mobil represents an engaging risk-reward opportunity. The technicals suggest upside, and the company has enough fundamental strength that provides sustainability comfort. Given where shares are trading currently, the company actually represents a very viable entry point before the expected breakout to higher price targets.

data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Precigen, ONEOK, Bitmine And More
data.description:
The Undercovered Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today, we're looking at articles published between Aug. 15–Aug. 21.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: Are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

CGDV Is Beating The S&P 500 By 70%, More Gains Are Ahead

Komal Sarwar | Buy

The fiscal year 2025 is turning out to be one of the best years for the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV) shareholders, which is in line with my previous analysis.

Year to date, the actively managed dividend value fund outshined the S&P 500 by 70%. The fund's current portfolio structure also positions it to deliver market-beating returns. Moreover, the management's smart portfolio strategy makes it a worth considering investment vehicle for the long term. Therefore, I reiterate my buy rating on CGDV.

DIVO: The ETF Built For Retirement's Most Dangerous Risk

GP Sigma Analytics | Buy

The Amplify CWP Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (NYSEARCA:DIVO) is one of the most misunderstood income focused ETF. Critics call it an overpriced covered call fund, growth investors skip it due to low dividend growth, and income seekers ignore it for higher-yielding alternatives. The reason behind this lies in a fundamental lack of understanding as to what DIVO truly is: a tactical blend strategy that fills the gap between growth and income in a manner not possible by traditional asset allocation.

Having considered the structure of DIVO, its performance in both up and down market cycles and its present positioning, I would say that it is one of the best solutions to the sequence of returns risk problem: the risk of losing money early in retirement that can destroy the portfolios regardless of long-term market performance.

Although not suitable to all investors, DIVO helps to fulfill a much needed gap in portfolio construction, which becomes more desirable as conventional 60/40 portfolio allocation struggles with structural challenges like chronic inflation and bond market volatility.

ONEOK: Now Is The Time To Load Up

Kody's Dividends | Buy

As an investor, I focus on business fundamentals and data. Not only that, but I also appreciate the importance of how human psychology factors into the equation.

To understand why each is important requires a look into how total returns are generated. The simple formula is as follows:

  • Dividends + earnings growth rate + valuation multiple movement = total returns

Business fundamentals are key because they directly support the former two inputs. A safe dividend from a business that's steadily growing has the potential to deliver solid long-term returns.

This is especially the case if the business is priced at a valuation that implies less growth than it ultimately delivers. In other words, lower expectations (i.e., bearish sentiment) give a company an easier bar to clear. That can create room for multiple expansions to further drive total returns.

This brings me to the focus of today, which is ONEOK (NYSE:OKE). When I last covered OKE with a buy rating in June, I appreciated OKE's future growth picture via a combination of synergies and projects being placed into service. The company's financial health was another plus. Lastly, the stock was moderately undervalued.

Today, I'm reiterating my buy rating. OKE continues to expect synergies from recent acquisitions. The company also unveiled plans for a major new Permian plant. OKE is progressing toward its targeted leverage ratio as well. Clinching the buy case for the stock, I believe shares are considerably undervalued.

Viking Therapeutics: Still On Track
